metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for the eval spec (e.g. `rebar eval src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/enrich.eval.yaml --assert-pass`) and state the pass threshold so the AC is mechanically verifiable in CI.
findings.finding: AC6's eval-spec component ('the eval spec uses independently-authored ground truth on ≥6 tickets … asserting the digest names the core problem + ≥1 correct entity') has no runnable proving command that produces a machine-readable pass/fail signal.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC6 second clause: 'the eval spec uses independently-authored ground truth (no self-oracle) on ≥6 tickets incl. ~4000-token bodies, asserting the digest names the core problem + ≥1 correct entity'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the prose description with: 'verified by `rebar eval src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/enrich.eval.yaml`; the spec must contain ≥6 hand-authored fixtures and all assertions must pass (score ≥ threshold T).' If `rebar eval` does not yet exist, call this out as a dependency or defer to a named child ticket with rationale.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Split AC6 into two bullets: one citing `pytest tests/unit/test_enrich.py::test_propositions_count_bounded` for the MIN/MAX enforcement, and one for the eval spec ground-truth requirement.
findings.finding: AC6 conflates two independent verifications — (a) propositions count bounded by configurable MIN/MAX and (b) eval spec uses independently-authored ground truth on ≥6 tickets — in a single bullet with no `pytest` test citation for (a), unlike every other AC which cites a specific test. The described deliverable `overlap_propositions_min/max` config has no dedicated unit-test AC.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, bullet 6 (final bullet)
findings.suggested_fix: AC6a: `propositions count is within [PROPOSITIONS_MIN, PROPOSITIONS_MAX]; verified by pytest tests/unit/test_enrich.py::test_propositions_count_bounded (mock LLM returning out-of-range list triggers validation error).` AC6b: `eval spec enrich.eval.yaml uses independently-authored ground truth on ≥6 tickets incl. ~4000-token bodies, asserting core problem + ≥1 correct entity.`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how runtime `VerifyConfig.overlap_propositions_min/max` values are threaded into the `ticket_digest` Pydantic validator (e.g. a config-parameterized factory, a module-level default that `enrich()` overrides via a closure, or validation moved into `enrich.py` post-schema-validation rather than inside the Pydantic model).
findings.finding: The config-driven `PROPOSITIONS_MIN/MAX` bound in `VerifyConfig` cannot be statically consumed by the zero-arg Pydantic model builder that `register_contract` stores — the builder is a `Callable[[], type]` with no config parameter — yet the plan requires the bound to be runtime-configurable. The processing protocol for threading the config into the structured-output validator is never defined.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Config: add overlap_propositions_min/max to VerifyConfig … so downstream S5/S6 can tune granularity'; contracts.py:register_contract signature `builder: Callable[[], type]`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the exemplar reference: point to `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/passes.py` (or a workflow `RunnerAgentStep` step) as the canonical model for `RunRequest(mode="structured", execution_mode="single_turn")`, since `completion.py`/`operations.py` are agentic ops that do not use this pattern.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the new op is 'Modeled on `src/rebar/llm/completion.py` / `src/rebar/llm/operations.py` (assemble context → load prompt → RunRequest(mode="structured", execution_mode="single_turn") → validate)' — but reading both named files shows neither uses `mode="structured"` nor `execution_mode` on their `RunRequest`. Both are agentic ops using the default `mode="findings"`. The `single_turn` + `mode="structured"` pattern lives in `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/passes.py` and the workflow engine (`RunnerAgentStep`). The named exemplars are the wrong reference for the construction the plan prescribes.
findings.location: ## What — 'Modeled on `src/rebar/llm/completion.py` / `src/rebar/llm/operations.py` (assemble context → load prompt → RunRequest(mode="structured", execution_mode="single_turn") → validate)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the ## What section, change 'Modeled on `src/rebar/llm/completion.py` / `src/rebar/llm/operations.py` (assemble context → load prompt → RunRequest(mode="structured", execution_mode="single_turn") → validate)' to 'Modeled on `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/passes.py` (the existing single_turn+structured pattern: assemble context → load prompt → RunRequest(mode="structured", execution_mode="single_turn", output_schema="ticket_digest") → validate via `contracts.response_model_for`)'. The review/completion ops are agentic and do not demonstrate the required construction.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add named pytest invocations for ACs 2, 4, and 5 (e.g. `pytest tests/unit/test_enrich.py::test_logs_excluded`, `::test_malformed_output`, `::test_llm_disabled`) so every AC has a concrete proving command.
findings.finding: ACs 2, 4, and 5 describe what the test must assert but supply no named pytest invocation, leaving their proving commands implicit and non-executable as written.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 ('verified by a fixture ticket containing a stack trace'), AC4 ('verified by a FakeRunner test returning bad JSON'), AC5 ('Disabled/absent-LLM path raises/reports cleanly')
findings.suggested_fix: AC2: add `verified by pytest tests/unit/test_enrich.py::test_logs_excluded`; AC4: `::test_malformed_output_raises_clean`; AC5: `::test_llm_disabled_raises_clean`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Scope section that the `ticket_digest` Pydantic contract in `contracts.py` must include a `field_validator` (or `Annotated[list, Field(min_length=..., max_length=...)]`) that enforces the PROPOSITIONS_MIN/MAX bounds at structured-output validation time, consistent with the codebase pattern documented in `structured.py` ('bounds in the validators, NOT the JSON Schema').
findings.finding: The plan specifies that propositions count is bounded by the configurable PROPOSITIONS_MIN/MAX but does not specify HOW these bounds are enforced in the Pydantic structured-output contract. The codebase's structured.py documents that 'numeric/length BOUNDS [must live] in the validators (NOT the JSON Schema, to stay inside Anthropic's strict-grammar subset)'. Without specifying that the contract's Pydantic model includes a field_validator (or min_length/max_length on the List field) that reads PROPOSITIONS_MIN/MAX at validation time, the bounds are unenforced at runtime — an LLM returning 1 or 10 propositions passes validate_structured() silently.
findings.location: Scope section (digest schema definition) and AC: 'propositions count is bounded by the configurable PROPOSITIONS_MIN/MAX (default 2..6)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify that the `PROPOSITIONS_MIN/MAX` bounds are enforced in the Pydantic response model's `@field_validator` (mirroring the stack's documented constraint placement in `structured.py`) rather than (or in addition to) the JSON Schema, so the bound holds on the Anthropic PromptedOutput path.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that `propositions` count is bounded by `PROPOSITIONS_MIN/MAX`, but does not state that these bounds must live in the Pydantic model validators (not the JSON Schema). The existing structured-output stack explicitly documents that 'numeric/length BOUNDS [go] in the validators (NOT the JSON Schema, to stay inside Anthropic's strict-grammar subset)' (structured.py lines 20–21). If the author places the min/max constraints only in `ticket_digest.schema.json` rather than in the Pydantic response model's validators, the bounds are silently unenforced on the Anthropic/PromptedOutput path — the exact failure mode the stack was designed to prevent.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — '`propositions` count is bounded by the configurable `PROPOSITIONS_MIN/MAX`'; What section — digest schema definition; src/rebar/llm/structured.py lines 20–21
findings.suggested_fix: In `contracts.py`, add a `@field_validator('propositions')` on the `TicketDigest` model that raises `ValueError` if `len(v) < PROPOSITIONS_MIN or len(v) > PROPOSITIONS_MAX`, following the pattern established by `CompletionVerdict._norm_verdict`. The JSON Schema can still document the bound, but the Pydantic validator is the enforcement layer.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `ticket_digest` Pydantic contract model body in the plan (field types, `@field_validator` for `propositions` count, and how PROPOSITIONS_MIN/MAX from config are threaded into the validator at call time).
findings.finding: The Pydantic model that `register_contract('ticket_digest', builder)` must return is never specified. The plan defines the JSON Schema fields but omits the model class body, including how `propositions` count bounds (PROPOSITIONS_MIN..PROPOSITIONS_MAX) are enforced in the validator — the framework pattern (structured.py:19-21, contracts.py:CompletionVerdict) puts bounds in Pydantic `@field_validator`s, not the JSON Schema, but the plan provides no validator body for the `ticket_digest` contract model.
findings.location: Scope section: 'register the structured response model in src/rebar/llm/contracts.py via register_contract("ticket_digest", …)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sub-section to 'Scope' specifying the builder: a zero-arg callable returning a Pydantic BaseModel with `problem_keywords: list[str]`, `component_or_area: str`, `key_entities: list[str]`, `propositions: list[str]` fields, and a `@field_validator('propositions')` that checks `PROPOSITIONS_MIN <= len(v) <= PROPOSITIONS_MAX` — either with the defaults baked in (and overridden at the call site before construction) or via a factory that closes over the config values.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact `category` value for `enrich.md` front-matter (e.g. `category: enrichment` or another non-`review` value) so the prompt is not inadvertently enrolled as a reviewer in the catalog.
findings.finding: The `category` front-matter value for `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/enrich.md` is never specified. The plan says the front-matter must include `category` but does not state its value. Tool-verified: `build_prompt_index` only indexes prompts where `category == 'review'` into the reviewer catalog, and enforces the single-default invariant only on those; a non-review category (e.g. `category: enrichment`) is silently skipped. Using `category: review` would incorrectly enroll `enrich` as a reviewer (wrong dimension, breaks catalog invariants); omitting it means `category=None` and `is_reviewer=False`. The executor has no spec to guide this choice.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Prompt file at the canonical location src/rebar/llm/reviewers/enrich.md … with front-matter … + category'
findings.suggested_fix: Set `category: enrichment` (or a new `category: extractor` value) — any string other than `review` keeps `is_reviewer=False` and skips the catalog invariant checks while still being parseable by `get_prompt`.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the intended `category` value (or enumerate valid options from `src/rebar/llm/prompting/prompts.py`) for the `enrich.md` front-matter so the executor can write it without investigation.
findings.finding: The prompt front-matter includes a `category` field but the plan never states what value to assign it; no valid `category` enum is cited, leaving the executor to guess or look it up.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Prompt file at the canonical location src/rebar/llm/reviewers/enrich.md … with front-matter … + category'
findings.suggested_fix: Add the concrete value inline, e.g. `category: enrichment` (or whatever the valid enum member is), matching the pattern used by existing reviewer prompts.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a locatable reference (file path, PR link, or issue number) to the epic-level Cupid-digest ADR in the Docs section, or add a stub ADR file (e.g., `docs/adr/0NNN-cupid-digest.md`) with at minimum the decision, context, and consequences captured before this story is merged.
findings.finding: The Cupid-digest architectural decision is deferred to 'the epic level (see epic Success Criteria)' with no locatable path, file, or link. A reviewer (or future maintainer) cannot verify that the ADR exists, covers the Cupid pattern as applied here, or has been written at all. This leaves the novel architectural decision effectively undocumented in any reachable artifact.
findings.location: ## Docs section; also ## Acceptance Criteria (no ADR AC)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Docs section: 'Epic-level ADR lives at `docs/adr/0NNN-cupid-digest.md`; that file must exist (even as a stub) before this story is closed.' Add a corresponding AC: `- [ ] \'docs/adr/0NNN-cupid-digest.md\' exists and covers the Cupid-pattern rationale (no-vector-DB, content-hash keying, LLM-as-extractor).`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend an existing test) verifying that `enrich.md` loads without PromptError and its front-matter declares `execution_mode: single_turn`, `outputs: ticket_digest`.
findings.finding: The prompt file `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/enrich.md` is a described deliverable with failure-prone constraints (valid `execution_mode` enum, `outputs: ticket_digest`, `category` front-matter — the plan explicitly warns `1-TURN` is invalid and raises PromptError), yet no AC verifies the prompt file is parseable and has correct front-matter.
findings.location: Scope section (prompt file requirements); Acceptance Criteria (all bullets)
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: 'The prompt file parses without PromptError and declares execution_mode: single_turn and outputs: ticket_digest; verified by pytest tests/unit/test_enrich.py::test_prompt_front_matter.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a pre-call input-size guard (e.g., max-token truncation or a hard character limit with a logged warning) in `enrich.py` before issuing RunRequest, so arbitrarily large ticket bodies cannot cause context errors or unbounded LLM cost.
findings.finding: The plan passes the full ticket body to the LLM with no pre-call input-size truncation guard. Discarding logs/stack-traces/code is a prompt instruction, not a pre-call filter — the entire raw ticket (potentially many thousands of tokens) is still sent, risking context-limit errors and unbounded per-call cost on large tickets.
findings.location: What section / Digest schema description: 'Logs, stack traces, and code are discarded by the prompt'
findings.suggested_fix: In `enrich.py`, before constructing the RunRequest, measure the ticket body length (e.g., via a token estimator or a conservative character ceiling like 32K chars) and truncate/warn if it exceeds a configurable `ENRICH_MAX_INPUT_TOKENS` (default e.g. 8000 tokens). Document this limit in the prompt front-matter and the config schema.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `overlap_propositions_min/max` should be hardcoded in the prompt/schema for S1 (YAGNI — S5/S6 are out of scope) and, if configuration is truly needed now, place the keys in `[tool.rebar.llm]` (the existing reserved LLM-layer section in `_config_schema.py`) rather than `VerifyConfig`, which is a gate-control stdlib section with no LLM-op tuning precedent.
findings.finding: The two new `overlap_propositions_min/max` config keys are placed in `VerifyConfig` (`[tool.rebar][verify]`) — a stdlib-core section with no LLM deps — but they parameterize LLM extraction granularity, not a verification gate. Every other `verify.*` key controls a gate behavior (require_signature_for_close, enable_code_review, etc.). The plan's own justification is 'downstream S5/S6 can tune granularity' — stories not yet delivered. Hardcoding 2..6 in the prompt/schema satisfies the current done-definition (S1 only); the config knobs are YAGNI until S5/S6 arrive. Additionally, placing LLM-layer tuning in the core stdlib config section violates the existing layering contract: `[tool.rebar.llm]` is already the designated reserved section for LLM-op parameters (`_RESERVED_SECTIONS = frozenset({'llm', 'snapshot'}`)).
findings.location: Scope section / `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` VerifyConfig / `[tool.rebar][verify]`
findings.suggested_fix: For S1: hardcode PROPOSITIONS_MIN=2 / PROPOSITIONS_MAX=6 as constants in `src/rebar/llm/enrich.py` and reference them in the prompt/schema. Defer config exposure to the S5/S6 story that actually consumes tunability. If pre-exposure is intentional, add the keys to the `[tool.rebar.llm]` reserved section (resolved by `rebar.llm.LLMConfig`) rather than `VerifyConfig`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/schemas/__init__.py` to the Scope edit targets and specify how `ticket_digest` is registered (e.g., a constant `TICKET_DIGEST = 'ticket_digest'` plus an entry in `CONTRACT_SCHEMAS` or an analogous exempt set, matching the pattern used for `completion_verdict`).
findings.finding: `src/rebar/schemas/__init__.py` is an unenumerated consumer: every `.schema.json` file added to `src/rebar/schemas/` must be registered in this module (under `OUTPUT_SCHEMAS`, `INPUT_SCHEMAS`, or `CONTRACT_SCHEMAS`) or an existing 'every-schema-file-is-wired' guard test will fail. The plan creates `ticket_digest.schema.json` but names no edit to `schemas/__init__.py`.
findings.location: Scope section — `src/rebar/schemas/ticket_digest.schema.json`
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: 'Edit `src/rebar/schemas/__init__.py`: add `TICKET_DIGEST = "ticket_digest"` constant and register it in `CONTRACT_SCHEMAS` (it is a prompt output contract consumed directly, not a command `--output`), mirroring `code_review_base_output`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Move `overlap_propositions_min`/`overlap_propositions_max` from `VerifyConfig` (`src/rebar/_config_schema.py`) to the LLM-layer config (`src/rebar/llm/config.py` / `LLMConfig`) so downstream S5/S6 LLM ops read them through the same `LLMConfig` they already consume, preserving the core/llm layering invariant documented in `_config_schema.py`.
findings.finding: The plan places overlap_propositions_min/max in VerifyConfig (_config_schema.py), which is the core stdlib config layer explicitly kept free of llm-layer concerns. The module header of _config_schema.py states 'llm.* keys live in the optional rebar.llm layer (not here) so the stdlib core never depends on the agents extra.' Downstream S5/S6 are LLM operations that will consume these bounds through LLMConfig, not through the core VerifyConfig — placing them in VerifyConfig either requires S5/S6 to import across the core/llm boundary (breaking the layering invariant) or creates a duplicated/inconsistent lookup path.
findings.location: Scope section: 'add overlap_propositions_min/max to VerifyConfig (src/rebar/_config_schema.py, [tool.rebar][verify], new overlap_* fields)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add overlap_propositions_min: int = 2 and overlap_propositions_max: int = 6 fields to LLMConfig in src/rebar/llm/config.py (alongside existing LLM-layer tuning params), with env-override support via REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_PROPOSITIONS_MIN/MAX following the existing LLMConfig env-override pattern. Remove them from the VerifyConfig plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in `enrich.py` (or confirm via inherited infrastructure) that transient LLM API errors are retried with exponential backoff, and add at least one structured log event / metric emission on LLM call failure so operators can observe enrichment error rates.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no retry/backoff strategy for transient LLM API failures in `enrich.py`, and names no observability instrumentation (metrics, structured log events, or traces) for the new LLM call failure point.
findings.location: Testing section / AC#4-5 / 'Modeled on src/rebar/llm/completion.py / src/rebar/llm/operations.py'
findings.suggested_fix: If `completion.py` already provides retry/backoff, add an explicit note in the plan ('enrich.py inherits retry/backoff from RunRequest infrastructure'). If not, add a retry decorator (e.g., tenacity with max 3 attempts, exponential backoff on 429/5xx) and emit a structured log entry (e.g., `logger.warning('enrich_llm_failure', ticket_id=…, attempt=…, error=…)`) on each failure and final exhaustion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm or specify that `register_contract` is idempotent (no-op on re-registration of the same key+schema) or raises a clear error on conflicting re-registration, and document this guarantee in `contracts.py`.
findings.finding: `register_contract('ticket_digest', …)` mutates a global registry in `contracts.py`, but the plan does not specify whether this registration is idempotent under multiple calls (e.g., repeated module imports, test teardown/re-import, or accidental double-registration).
findings.location: Scope section: 'register the structured response model in src/rebar/llm/contracts.py via register_contract("ticket_digest", …)'
findings.suggested_fix: In `contracts.py`, implement `register_contract` as: if key already registered with the identical model, no-op; if registered with a different model, raise `ContractConflictError`. This makes both test isolation and accidental double-registration safe.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `enrich.md` belongs in `reviewers/` (the reviewer directory) or a separate `prompts/` directory for non-reviewer structured-extraction prompts, and enumerate `reviewers/index.json` (or the prompt index tests) as affected if the former.
findings.finding: Placing `enrich.md` inside `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/` makes it subject to the prompt-index regeneration machinery (`regenerate_prompt_index` scans this directory for all `.md` files). The plan does not state whether a non-reviewer prompt in `reviewers/` is valid, whether it will be added to `index.json`, or whether the prompt-index invariant tests will pass for a prompt without `category: review` living in that directory.
findings.location: Scope section — `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/enrich.md`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief prior-art comparison to `## Why` explaining what the existing `spec_scan` / `spec-alignment` batch reviewer does and does NOT cover, and why the per-ticket Cupid digest is needed in addition (not instead).
findings.finding: The plan does not consider the existing `spec_scan` / `spec-alignment` reviewer as prior art for semantic overlap detection. `spec_scan.py` + the `spec-alignment` prompt already finds 'scope overlaps' between epics/tickets using an LLM; the plan introduces a new extraction op without explaining why the existing overlap-detection path doesn't cover the paraphrase-detection use case, or what the Cupid digest adds that extending `spec_scan` would not.
findings.location: ## Why / ## What (plan motivation section)
findings.suggested_fix: In `## Why`, add: 'Note: the existing `spec_scan` / `spec-alignment` reviewer detects overlap across a batch of epics at scan time, but does not produce a per-ticket canonical digest that downstream S5/S6 can query pairwise on-demand without re-running the full LLM batch. The Cupid digest addresses this gap by amortizing extraction once per ticket edit so pairwise queries are O(1).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the unverified capability assertion that lexical matching on `propositions[]` catches paraphrase: either cite evidence/eval results, specify the exact matching algorithm S5/S6 will use on proposition strings, or replace `propositions[]` with a field type (e.g. normalized keyphrases) that is actually amenable to the lexical matching strategy the Cupid paper uses.
findings.finding: The plan's core novelty claim — that lexical matching on LLM-generated `propositions[]` (free-text sentences) will catch paraphrase like 'login broken' vs 'users can't authenticate' — is asserted but unresolved. The cited Cupid paper (arXiv:2308.10022) uses extracted keyphrases + BM25/Jaccard for lexical matching, not free-text proposition sentences. Proposition sentences generated by the LLM will vary in wording across runs, making 'cheap lexical match forever after' unreliable unless the downstream matching strategy (S5/S6/S7) accounts for this variation.
findings.location: ## What (digest schema + propositions field description)

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
